title: Prologue & Episodes I–II: Betrayal
paragraphs: CHARACTERS: MEDEA, a woman of Colchis. JASON, her husband. CREON, King of Corinth. AEGEUS, King of Athens. A NURSE. A TUTOR. CHILDREN of Medea and Jason. CHORUS of Corinthian Women.
PROLOGUE
(Before MEDEA's house in Corinth. The NURSE speaks.)

NURSE: How I wish that the ship Argo had never flown through the dark Clashing Rocks, and never come to Colchis—never fetched the Golden Fleece! For then my mistress Medea would not have sailed to the towers of Iolcos, her heart struck with love for Jason. Then she would not have persuaded the daughters of Pelias to kill their father. Then she would not have fled with Jason here to Corinth.

She gave up everything for him. She left her home, her country, her family. She used her arts to save him—her knowledge of magic and medicine—and she bore him children. And now he has thrown her over. He is going to marry the daughter of Creon, king of Corinth. Medea is cast out—a foreign woman, alone, in a country not her own.
She does not weep. She does not beg. She stares into the darkness and is silent, and that silence frightens me more than weeping.
EPISODE I
(CREON approaches.)

CREON: You there—Medea—with the angry look, and the anger against your husband—I have come to tell you plainly: you are banished. Take your children and go. My decree is final. I fear you, Medea. You are a clever woman, you know evil arts—
MEDEA: Fear me? King Creon, why? What harm have I done you?

CREON: I'll be honest. I fear your wits. I fear the hurt you may do my daughter. Word has come to me that you have threatened harm to Jason and his bride and me. I know you have the knowledge to carry out such threats.
MEDEA: I see. (She pauses.) Grant me one day. One single day to arrange my affairs, to find a place of refuge for my children. One day.
CREON: (reluctantly) One day. Then go.
(He leaves.)
MEDEA: (to herself) A fool. He gave me time. I needed only a day to put my plans in motion.
CHORUS: Medea—what will you do?
MEDEA: The general rule is this: a woman who has been wronged should weep and endure. But there are those whose nature is other. I am of that nature. They have wronged me. They will all regret it.
EPISODE II
(JASON comes to speak with her.)

JASON: Medea. I see that rage has not improved your judgment. Be reasonable. I have done what was necessary. The marriage to Creon's daughter gives us both protection in this city—gives our children protection—

MEDEA: (with controlled fury) You have the face to come before me. Was it not you who brought me here? Was it not I who saved your life—killed the dragon—helped you steal the Fleece? For you I killed my own brother. I gave up everything. My country. My family. My future. For you.
JASON: The gods helped you more than I did.
MEDEA: The gods. I see. And this new marriage—that is also the gods' arrangement?
JASON: It is advantageous. You are clever. Try to understand advantage.
MEDEA: (very quietly) Go. I have heard enough.
(JASON leaves.)
CHORUS: When Love comes with excess—it brings no honor to man. But if the goddess comes gently, no other grace is more desirable.

title: Episodes III–V: The Plan
paragraphs: EPISODE III
(AEGEUS, King of Athens, passes through Corinth on his way from Delphi.)

MEDEA: Aegeus—you are a friend. I need your help. Give me sanctuary in Athens when I come—and I will use my arts to give you the son you have prayed for. Do this, and I swear I will never leave your city willingly.
AEGEUS: Come then to Athens. I will protect you there.
(He goes. MEDEA turns to the CHORUS.)

MEDEA: Now I have what I need. Now listen. I am going to send my children to Jason's bride with gifts—a robe and a golden crown. The gifts will be poisoned. She will die in agony. And Creon, when he touches her, will die too. Then—(She stops.)
CHORUS: What else?
MEDEA: (with great difficulty) Then I will kill my own children.
CHORUS: (horror) Medea—
MEDEA: They are mine. Jason shall not have them. He shall not have his heirs, his line, his future. He shall have nothing. This is my revenge.
EPISODE IV
(The CHILDREN have carried the gifts to the princess. They return with the TUTOR.)
TUTOR: Good news, mistress! The princess received your gifts with joy. Peace has been made.
(MEDEA weeps.)
CHILDREN: Mother—why do you weep, when there is good news?
MEDEA: (pulling them to her) I weep for joy. Come to me. Let me hold you. Let me look at you. (She embraces them.) How beautiful your hands are. And your faces. How beautiful.
CHILDREN: (sensing something) Mother—your eyes—
MEDEA: (releasing them) Go in. Go in, children. (They go inside.)

MEDEA: (alone) I cannot. Can I do what I have planned? No—let them live—let them go to another country—anywhere—I will spare them—But no. If I leave them they will fall into my enemies' hands; they will suffer more. And they are mine. Jason shall not have them. It must be done.
EPISODE V
(A MESSENGER rushes in.)
MESSENGER: Medea—fly! The gifts have done their work. The princess is dead—and the king—they are both dead. Fly, take ship, go anywhere. Take any refuge you can find.
MEDEA: Tell me how they died.

MESSENGER: When your children brought the gifts, the princess put on the robe and the golden crown and was delighted. But then—the poison began to work. Fire—a terrible fire—eating through flesh and bone. She fell. And the king, her father, when he knelt beside her and touched her—the poison claimed him too. They are both dead.
MEDEA: (quietly) Then everything is as it should be. Now—the last thing.
(She goes into the house. The CHORUS hears the children cry out.)
CHORUS: Do you hear those cries? The children—can nothing stop this?
(Silence. JASON rushes in.)
JASON: Where is Medea? Tell her she cannot hide from me—she killed the princess—she killed the king—
MEDEA: (appearing above the house, in a chariot drawn by dragons, with the bodies of the CHILDREN) I am here.
JASON: You—monster—how could you kill your own children?
MEDEA: To wound you. There was no other way to wound you deeply enough.
JASON: Give me the bodies. Let me bury them.
MEDEA: No. I will bury them myself. And I will go to Athens. You—Jason—live on, and grow old, and remember what love can do when it is betrayed.
(The chariot flies away.)
JASON: (alone) Zeus, do you see this? Do you see how the gods' justice works? (He falls silent.)

CHORUS: Many are the fates which Zeus in Olympus dispenses; many things the gods achieve beyond our expectations. What was anticipated does not come to pass. And the unexpected happens—so ends this story.
CURTAIN.
